does: This paper investigates whether explicitly injecting linguistic structure into language models improves their alignment with brain activity recordings from fMRI data. The authors modify transformer-based language models by biasing their attention mechanisms using annotations from syntactic formalisms like dependency parsing and semantic formalisms like minimal recursion semantics. They evaluate alignment across two separate fMRI datasets by measuring how well model representations correlate with neural recordings. The results show significant improvements in alignment for one dataset when structure is incorporated, while the second dataset exhibits more mixed outcomes. The paper provides detailed analysis of when and why linguistic structure helps explain brain activity, quantifying the benefits and limitations of this approach.
connects: This work builds on the established neuroscience practice of evaluating neural network models against brain recordings to understand language processing mechanisms. It directly engages with recent debates about whether language models learn linguistic structure implicitly without supervision, extending prior work by Manning et al. and others investigating emergent structure in unsupervised models. The paper responds to questions raised by Warstadt and Bowman about whether symbolic linguistic theories remain relevant for cognitive modeling in the era of deep learning. It combines techniques from both computational linguistics—using established syntactic and semantic annotation schemes—with neuroscientific methods, bridging the divide between formal linguistic theory and empirical neural modeling.
enables: This research enables more precise scientific hypotheses about how the brain constructs linguistic meaning by allowing neuroscientists to test whether specific compositional mechanisms matter for neural alignment. The framework of injecting targeted linguistic biases into language models provides a systematic way to evaluate competing theories of language representation in the brain. Future work could extend this approach to test other formal linguistic frameworks, investigate individual differences in linguistic processing, or explore how different types of structure (phonological, pragmatic, discourse) contribute to neural explanations. The methodology also opens possibilities for understanding which aspects of explicit linguistic structure are cognitively relevant versus merely epiphenomenal.
